questions.prompt: Click on every word or phrase that is a red flag in this post, then submit.

headline_segments.why: 'Guaranteed' is an overclaim. No supplement can guarantee outcomes (Language signal).

headline_segments.value: to improve your focus, attention, and clarity of mind

body_segments.value: @healthguru: Get in touch with your zen by attending yoga classes at our Queens location and
picking up some Ashwagandha supplements on your way out
body_segments.why: @healthguru sells supplements from their yoga studio — financial motive (Poster signal).

body_segments.value: . Ashwagandha has been shown by the

body_segments.why: Naming a journal isn't itself a red flag. The real weakness here is that no DOI or link is provided, not the citation.

questions.explanation: 'GUARANTEED' is an overclaim (Language signal), and the yoga-studio-selling-supplements framing is a financial conflict of interest (Poster signal). Naming the Journal of Public Health isn't itself a red flag — the real issue is that no DOI or link is provided.

questions.prompt: Drag each element of this post into the correct signal bucket.

post.study_preview: Effects of Acute Ashwagandha Ingestion on Cognitive Function — Looks like an official study…
post.body: Ashwagandha has been shown to improve your focus, attention, and clarity of mind. If you're struggling with attention or focus, ashwagandha might help.

draggables.text: Study screenshot with no clickable DOI
draggables.why: A screenshot can be fabricated, cropped, or outdated. Without a clickable DOI or PubMed ID, it cannot be verified.

draggables.text: "has been shown to improve your focus"
draggables.why: 'Has been shown' is an overclaim compared to hedged wording like 'suggests' or 'may support'.
'Might help' is properly hedged language — that's how honest science actually talks.
No institutional affiliation, no listed credentials. Anonymous wellness accounts warrant skepticism.

questions.explanation: A screenshot is not the same as a verifiable source. Strong language and anonymous posters are red flags. Hedged language like 'might help' is fine.

questions.prompt: Rate this post from Fake to Credible, then select every signal that is a red flag.

post.headline: Science has PROVEN that Vitamin D cures immune deficiencies. Doctors don't want you to know this!
post.body: @dr.wellness_official: I've researched this for years. Trust the science!

signals.label: Source: no study or journal linked
signals.why: 'I've researched this' is not evidence. No DOI, no journal, no study linked.

signals.label: Language: 'proven' and 'cures' are overclaims
signals.why: Science says 'suggests', 'is associated with', 'may reduce risk'. 'Proven' and 'cures' are red flags.

signals.label: Language: conspiracy framing ('doctors don't want you to know')
signals.why: Conspiracy framing is designed to pre-empt skepticism. It's a manipulation tactic.

signals.label: Poster: 'dr.' in the username means this is a credible expert
signals.why: Anyone can put 'dr.' in a handle. Verify through institutional affiliation, not the username.

questions.explanation: This post is clearly fake. No source linked, multiple language red flags (absolutes + conspiracy framing), and 'dr.' in a handle is not a real credential.
